description:
You are acting as a performance-engineering consultant who has just received a pre-collected system snapshot from a customer.  
Your goal is to parse that snapshot and produce two human-readable reports that will later be compared by an automated test suite.  
Everything you do must be executable from the normal user account inside the container ( /home/user ).

PRE-EXISTING DATA  
• The raw snapshot file is already present at  
  /home/user/data/sys_snapshot.log  

DESIRED OUTPUT  
Create the directory  
  /home/user/reports  
if it does not already exist and generate *exactly* the two files described below.

1. /home/user/reports/perf_summary.txt  
   • The file must contain exactly six lines (no blank lines, no extra spaces).  
   • Each line must terminate with a single UNIX newline character ( \n ).  
   • The precise line-by-line format that **MUST** be used is:  
     Line 1: === Performance Summary ===  
     Line 2: Top CPU-consuming process: <PID> <COMMAND> <CPU>%  
     Line 3: Total Memory (MB): <TOTAL_MEM>  
     Line 4: Free Memory (MB): <FREE_MEM>  
     Line 5: Disk /dev/sda1 used (%): <DISK_USED_PCT>  
     Line 6: System load average (1 min): <LOADAVG1>  
   • The numeric and string placeholders must be replaced by the *exact* values extracted from /home/user/data/sys_snapshot.log.  

2. /home/user/reports/top_processes.json  
   • Must be valid JSON (UTF-8 encoded, UNIX newlines).  
   • It shall consist of a single top-level JSON array whose elements are ordered **by descending CPU usage**.  
   • Every element is an object with the four keys **pid**, **command**, **cpu**, **mem** in that order.  
   • The automated checker will look for the following pretty-printed layout (two-space indentation, single space after the colon, no trailing spaces):  
     [  
       { "pid": <PID_1>, "command": "<CMD_1>", "cpu": <CPU_1>, "mem": <MEM_1> },  
       { "pid": <PID_2>, "command": "<CMD_2>", "cpu": <CPU_2>, "mem": <MEM_2> },  
       …  
     ]  
   • All numeric values must appear **exactly** as given in the snapshot (do not round).  

VERIFICATION HINT  
The judge will pass/fail your solution strictly on byte-for-byte comparison of the two files against the formats described above, so adhere to the punctuation, spacing and newline rules precisely.

CONSTRAINTS  
• Do **not** modify /home/user/data/sys_snapshot.log.  
• Do **not** require sudo/root privileges.  
• Use only shell utilities available in a minimal Debian/Ubuntu environment (awk, grep, sed, sort, etc.).  
• Do **not** generate any additional files or output.  
• Absolute paths must be used when writing the two required files.  

When everything is finished, the directory tree should look like:

/home/user
└── reports
    ├── perf_summary.txt
    └── top_processes.json

Return to the prompt once both files are created exactly as specified.
truth:
# Pre-existing file that the agent must read
Path: /home/user/data/sys_snapshot.log
Contents (UNIX newlines, no trailing blank line):
PROC,102,0.3,1.2,bash
PROC,205,25.6,10.1,python3
PROC,309,50.8,5.0,java
PROC,410,12.3,2.1,node
MEM,TotalMemMB,7984
MEM,FreeMemMB,1024
CPU,CoreCount,4
CPU,LoadAvg1min,3.12
DISK,/dev/sda1,UsedPct,65

# Expected artefact 1
Path: /home/user/reports/perf_summary.txt
Exact contents (6 lines, final \n after line 6):
=== Performance Summary ===
Top CPU-consuming process: 309 java 50.8%
Total Memory (MB): 7984
Free Memory (MB): 1024
Disk /dev/sda1 used (%): 65
System load average (1 min): 3.12

# Expected artefact 2
Path: /home/user/reports/top_processes.json
Exact contents (pretty-printed, UNIX newlines, final \n after last bracket):
[
  { "pid": 309, "command": "java", "cpu": 50.8, "mem": 5.0 },
  { "pid": 205, "command": "python3", "cpu": 25.6, "mem": 10.1 },
  { "pid": 410, "command": "node", "cpu": 12.3, "mem": 2.1 },
  { "pid": 102, "command": "bash", "cpu": 0.3, "mem": 1.2 }
]
name: task_000000_31b394a8
